Support for 16 TB and 24 TB instances is currently available in selected AWS regions. For the latest information on supported regions and availability zones, please refer to the SAP HANA Cloud documentation.

 

Data Lake Innovations

Thin Clone Decoupling for Data Lake Relational Engine 

Building on the thin clone capability in SAP HANA Cloud, data lake relational engine, which is designed for creation and deployment of clones of petabyte-scale databases in development and testing environments in just a few minutes, we are introducing thin clone decoupling. Thin clone decoupling promotes a thin clone to a fully standalone SAP HANA Cloud, data lake relational engine instance. Once the decoupling process completes, the instance operates independently, completely detached from the parent instance. 

Upon decoupling, backups are enabled on the decoupled instance, making it eligible for disaster recovery scenarios. The decoupled instance can itself serve as a source for creating new thin clones. 

 Thin clone decoupling opens a clear path for promoting development or test databases to production-grade instances improving data reliability and integrity. 

 

Innovations on Calculation View features

The calculation view features blog for 2026 QRC2 by @jan_zwickel covers several new calculation view capabilities. Two complementary deployment options help to reduce deployment time and protect privileges: optimized redeployment skips unchanged dependent views instead of dropping and recreating them all, and the new replace option performs in-place replacement so that cross-container privileges are not lost.

On the scalability front, Active/Active read-enabled hints can be set at the individual calculation view level for fine-grained query routing to read replicas. A Join-to-Non-Equi-Join conversion in the context menu eliminates the need to rebuild join logic from scratch when switching from equi joins to non-equi joins. The support of calculated measures executed at query runtime allow defining runtime calculations for MDS Cubes in calculation views. Finally, a new status dialog surfaces the view's connection state, read-only reasons, data classification inconsistencies, and BDC integration status when a calculation view is opened.

 

 

Thanks for taking the time to explore the What’s New in SAP HANA Cloud on Q2 2026 innovations!

If you’d like to go deeper into the technical details of the Q2 2026 release, we recommend visiting the What’s New Viewer in the technical documentation. It provides a comprehensive overview of the full release scope. To stay informed about upcoming innovations, announcements, and best practices, follow the SAP HANA Cloud tag.

You can also find our latest blog posts by searching for the #whatsnewinsaphanacloud hashtag.

If you missed earlier What’s New webinars, you’ll find recordings of past sessions and upcoming events in our YouTube playlist. Our What’s New webinar covering the Q2 2026 innovations will be posted on this playlist very soon. You can also download the slides from the webinar here.

Do you have questions about SAP HANA Cloud or want to share your thoughts on the new features?
Join the conversation in the SAP HANA Cloud Community Q&A or leave a comment below. We’d love to hear from you!

entry.title: Code Connect 2026: Final Info Before We Kick Off!
entry.content:
Code Connect 2026 is just around the corner! From July 13–16, 2026, the SAP developer community will gather in St. Leon-Rot, Germany – onsite and online.

Here’s everything you need to know before we get started.

 

 

We Are Fully Booked!

All onsite tickets are fully booked - thank you for the amazing interest!

If you couldn’t secure a ticket:

  • Join the waiting lists on Code Connect 2026 in case seats become available.
  • Follow selected sessions via live stream or Microsoft Teams link.

Even if you're not onsite, you can still be part of Code Connect. Selected sessions will be streamed live, and recordings of some sessions will be available afterwards. Check back soon for details.

 

 

 

UI5con (July 14)

  • Main stage sessions will be live streamed on YouTube.
  • Check the agenda to see which sessions are streamed.

 

 

re>≡CAP (July 15)

  • Main stage sessions will be broadcasted.
  • Some sidetracks (W1/W2) can be attended via Microsoft Teams link.
  • Check the agenda to see which sessions are broadcasted or available via Microsoft Teams link.

 

 

HANA Tech Con (July 16)

  • The links will be announced on the event day.

 

The Agendas Are Live

The full program is already available - time to plan your schedule!

Check-In opens at 8.00 AM every day. Please bring the QR code with you that you can find in your ticket.

July 13 – Code Jams & Community Meetup

  • Hands-on Code Jams:
    • OpenUI5
    • CAP
    • AI Agents
  • Informal pre-event meetup (no registration needed):
    Ihle Besen, Höfe am Sträßel 3, 69231 Rauenberg
    5:00 PM CEST

July 14–16 – Main Conference Days

Explore all agendas:

  • July 14: UI5con
  • July 15: re>≡CAP
  • July 16: HANA Tech Con

On July 14 also the HANA AI CodeJam takes place.

 

Location

Code Connect takes place at:

SAP-Allee 27
68789 St. Leon-Rot, Germany

Find detailed information on directions, parking, and public transport here.

 

Final Tips

  • Bookmark your favorite sessions in advance
  • Arrive early for popular sessions (rooms fill quickly!)
  • Bring your laptop for Code Jams and hands-on sessions/ workshops
  • Don’t miss the networking moments - some of the best conversations happen in between sessions
  • Follow the agendas closely for last-minute updates

 

See You Soon!

We’re looking forward to an inspiring week of learning, sharing, and connecting.

See you at Code Connect 2026!

entry.title: Why Stock Fields Return Zero in Custom CDS Views on MARC - and How to Resolve It
entry.content:
Why Stock Fields Return Zero in Custom CDS Views on MARC - and How to Resolve It

I was building a custom CDS view on the MARC table (Plant Data for Material) to extract data into SAP Datasphere using Change Data Capture (CDC) for delta loads.

After activating the view, I checked the data and found something odd. The field TRAME (Stock in Transit) had a proper value in the MARC table, but in my CDS view the same field was completely empty.

To give a concrete example, for one material and plant the MARC table showed TRAME = 50.000, but my CDS view showed TRAME = 0.000 for the same key. All the master data fields were perfectly fine. Only the stock field was wrong.

That single clue — master data correct, stock field zero — is what pointed me to the real cause.


Where the data actually lives in S/4HANA

In the older SAP ECC system, stock quantities were physically saved in many tables, and the same figure was often repeated in several places. This created redundancy and slowed things down.

In S/4HANA, SAP redesigned this part of the system with the New Simplified Data Model (NSDM). The idea is straightforward: all stock movements are written into a single table called MATDOC, and the stock figures are calculated when you ask for them instead of being stored everywhere.

Because of this change, MARC became a so-called hybrid table. It still physically stores the material master and planning data, but the stock quantity fields are no longer filled in the table itself. Fields like TRAME, UMLMC, GLGMG and BWESB are now worked out on the fly from MATDOC whenever they are read.

So in S/4HANA, the TRAME column inside the physical MARC table is basically empty. The real stock-in-transit figure is calculated from MATDOC only at the moment it is needed.

Why SE16 showed the value but my CDS did not

This is the most important point of the whole story. SE16 and my CDS view were reading the same table through two completely different paths.

When you open MARC in SE16, the read goes through the normal application layer. SAP automatically redirects that read to a proxy object behind MARC, which performs the MATDOC calculation and returns the correct TRAME value. That is why SE16 happily showed 50.000.

My CDS view, on the other hand, was written as select from marc. A CDS view runs straight on the database and reads the physical MARC table directly. It never passes through the redirect, so it picked up the empty physical column and returned 0.000.

In one sentence:

SE16 reads MARC through the proxy and gets the calculated value. A CDS that selects directly from MARC reads the raw table and gets nothing. That difference in the read path was the entire reason the data "did not come."


My first fix — and why it failed

My first idea was simple. If the proxy returns the correct value, why not read from the proxy instead of the table? So I changed the source of my view to the proxy object:

Before (TRAME comes as zero):

@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZCDS_MARC_DEX'

@AbapCatalog.compiler.compareFilter: true

@AbapCatalog.preserveKey: true

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED

@EndUserText.label: 'Plant Data for Material'

@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true

@analytics.dataExtraction:{enabled: true,

delta.changeDataCapture:{mapping:[

{table:'marc',role:#MAIN,

viewElement:['Matnr','Werks'],

tableElement:['matnr','werks']}]}}

define view ZCDS_MARC_DEX_CDC as select from marc

{

   key matnr as Matnr,

   key werks as Werks,

   pstat as Pstat,

   lvorm as Lvorm,

   bwtty as Bwtty,

   xchar as Xchar,

   trame as Trame,

... remaining MARC fields ...

After (TRAME correct and CDC activates):

@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZCDS_MARC_DEX'

@AbapCatalog.compiler.compareFilter: true

@AbapCatalog.preserveKey: true

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED

@EndUserText.label: 'Plant Data for Material'

@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true 

@analytics:{

dataExtraction.enabled: true,

dataExtraction.delta.changeDataCapture:{

mapping:[

   {

      table:'marc',

      role:#MAIN,

      viewElement:['Matnr','Werks'],

      tableElement:['matnr','werks']

   }]

}

}

define view ZCDS_MARC_DEX_CDC as select from nsdm_e_marc

{

   key matnr as Matnr,

   key werks as Werks,

   pstat as Pstat,

   lvorm as Lvorm,

   bwtty as Bwtty, 

   xchar as Xchar,

   trame as Trame,

... remaining MARC fields ...

The only real change is the data source: from marc to nsdm_e_marc. The CDC mapping still refers to the physical table marc


This is not only a MARC problem

The same behaviour happens with all NSDM stock tables in S/4HANA. If your CDS reads stock fields straight from any of these, you can get zeros:

  • MARC – plant stock fields (TRAME, UMLMC, GLGMG, BWESB)
  • MARD – storage location stock
  • MCHB – batch stock
  • MKOL – special / consignment stock
  • MSKA – sales order stock
  • MSKU – customer special stock
  • MSLB – stock with vendor (subcontracting)
  • MSPR, MSSA, MSSQ – project and sales order stock totals
  • MSTB, MSTE, MSTQ – stock in transit tables


A simple rule of thumb: if a table held stock quantities in ECC, in S/4HANA those quantities are now calculated, and a direct CDS read will return zero. Use the table's proxy instead.

How to find the correct proxy in your own system

You do not have to guess the name. For any table (example: MARC):

  1. Open the table in SE11.
  2. Choose menu Extras → Replacement Object to see the assigned proxy (for MARC it is NSDM_E_MARC).

Example – "MARC" Replacement Object

Conclusion

What first looked like a bug in my CDS view turned out to be normal S/4HANA behaviour. Under the New Simplified Data Model, stock quantity fields are no longer stored in tables like MARC — they are calculated from MATDOC only when they are read. SE16 shows the correct value because it goes through the replacement object, while a CDS view that reads the table directly picks up the empty physical column and returns zero.

The fix itself is small but important: source the CDS view from the replacement object (for MARC, NSDM_E_MARC) instead of the base table. After that one change, TRAME returned the correct value. And because this is standard NSDM behaviour, the same approach works for every stock table in the family — whenever a stock field comes back as zero, the first thing to check is whether you are reading the base table directly instead of its replacement object.

The bigger lesson I took away is to always confirm where a field's data actually lives before building a CDS view on top of a table. A quick look at SE11 → Extras → Replacement Object tells you whether a table is a hybrid table and which object you should really be reading from. That single check can save a lot of confusing "the value is there in SE16 but missing in my view" situations.

entry.title: Calculation View Features of 2026 QRC2
entry.content:
Within the time frame of 2026 Q2, several new calculation view features have been released in SAP Business Application Studio when connected to SAP HANA Cloud database QRC2. Some of these features are highlighted below. You can find examples that illustrate the individual features here. An overview of features of other releases can be found here.

Optimized Redeployment

When a calculation view is redeployed in a stacked scenario, all dependent objects in the same HDI container are normally dropped and sequentially recreated. This default behavior avoids cascading revalidations but comes with a cost: privileges on dependent objects are temporarily, or in cross-container scenarios potentially permanently, revoked, and the full recreate cycle adds significant deployment time.

Optimized redeployment addresses this. Instead of dropping all dependents upfront, a consistency check determines which dependent views are still valid after the change. Views unaffected by the redeployed calculation view are kept as-is and skipped entirely. This reduces both the time spent recreating unaffected views and the privilege reassignment overhead.

To enable optimized redeployment for calculation views, add the following parameter to the hdi-deploy invocation in package.json:

"start": "node node_modules/@sap/hdi-deploy/deploy.js --parameter com.sap.hana.di.calculationview/optimized_redeploy=true"

A global parameter (optimized_redeploy=true) is also available and activates the behavior for all HDI object types that support it.

Replace Instead of Drop and Recreate During Redeployment

By default, calculation views are dropped and recreated during redeployment. Dropping a view revokes all privileges associated with it. Privileges defined in HDI roles of the same container are automatically regranted at the end of deployment, but direct privileges held by roles outside the current HDI container are permanently lost.

The new replace option substitutes the drop-and-recreate cycle with an in-place replacement. Because the object is never dropped, existing privileges are preserved throughout deployment. The benefit is most significant when combined with optimized redeployment: without it, dependent views are dropped before the root view is replaced, so they never benefit from the replace logic. With both options active, the replace strategy also applies to dependent views.

To enable the replace behavior, pass the following parameter:

"start": "node node_modules/@sap/hdi-deploy/deploy.js --parameter optimized_replace=true"

Combining optimized_replace=true with optimized_redeploy=true gives the strongest deployment time and privilege preservation benefit.

 

Active/Active Read-Enabled Hints

In an Active/Active (Read-Enabled) setup, query routing hints can now be configured at the individual calculation view level. This gives fine-grained control over whether a query is directed to the primary system or the read replica, allowing workload to be distributed and overall throughput to be increased.

 

Hints are evaluated only on calculation views that are directly referenced in the query. 

 

Switching from a Join Node to a Non-Equi Join Node

A Join node can now be converted directly to a Non-Equi Join node via the node's context menu, without needing to manually rebuild the join logic.

During conversion you can choose how the resulting join condition is expressed: either graphically, based on the column display,

or as an explicit join expression.

This simplifies the refactoring of join logic and reduces manual rework. Note that features not supported by Non-Equi Join nodes, such as dynamic joins, text joins, and filter expressions, are removed automatically during the conversion.

Calculation View Status Dialog

When opening a calculation view with certain properties, a status dialog now appears and reports details about the current view state. Where applicable, the dialog also offers direct actions to resolve issues, for example, prompting you to log in to Cloud Foundry when no active database connection is found.

The dialog covers four categories:

  • Read-only mode: reasons why the calculation view was opened in read-only mode
  • Database connection: current connection status to the database
  • Data classification inconsistencies: detected mismatches in the data classification setting
  • BDC integration: whether the calculation view is used with BDC integration mode

 

Calculated Measures in MDS Cubes

Calculated measures can now be added to MDS Cubes and are evaluated at query runtime. This makes it possible to execute calculations at query runtime.

If a calculated measure cannot be translated into a valid MDS formula, deployment fails with a corresponding error message.

entry.title: Connecting Two SAP CAP Projects Without Duplicating Your Data
entry.content:
There's a problem that comes up on almost every multi-project SAP BTP engagement, and I've seen teams solve it in some really messy ways.

You have two CAP projects. One of them — let's call it Project A — owns a set of shared constants, configuration parameters, maybe a job run log. Project B needs to read that data. So what do people do? They copy the table. Or they create an API. Or they hardcode values that really shouldn't be hardcoded.

All of these work. None of them are clean.

The proper solution is HDI cross-container synonyms. Once it's set up, Project B can query Project A's HANA tables directly — same SQL, same CAP service code, no replication, no drift between environments. It looks and feels like a local table. It just happens to live in someone else's HDI container.

I put together a Tech Bite on this recently and figured it's worth writing up properly, because the documentation is scattered and the first time you set it up you will almost certainly hit at least one of the three errors I mention at the end.

The thing nobody explains first: why it's complicated at all

HDI containers are isolated. That's the point of them — each CAP project gets its own schema, its own deploy lifecycle, its own access controls. But that isolation also means Container B can't just say "I want to read from Container A's schema" — because the schema name is dynamically assigned at deployment time. You can't hardcode it.

So SAP's solution is a User-Provided Service, or UPS. It's a simple Cloud Foundry construct — basically a key-value store with no runtime component — that carries the schema name of Project A. You register it with the -t hana tag, declare it in Project B's mta.yaml, and the HDI deployer picks it up during deployment and injects the correct schema wherever you've referenced it.

That's it. That's the whole trick. Everything else — the synonym files, the config files, the grants — is just plumbing on top of that core idea.

What you're actually building in Project B

Once you understand what the UPS is doing, the rest makes sense. You need four types of files:

.hdiconfig tells the deployer which plugins to use. You need two of these — one in db/src/ and one in db/cfg/. It's a small JSON file, easy to get wrong by forgetting one of the two locations.

.hdbsynonym is where you define the local alias. So instead of referencing PROJECT_A_SCHEMA.CONSTANTS_TABLE (which you can't hardcode anyway), you just write CONSTANTS_TABLE and map it to the actual technical object name in Project A. This lives in db/src/.

.hdbsynonymconfig is the file that connects the synonym to the UPS. This is what makes the schema resolution dynamic. It lives in db/cfg/ — deliberately separate from db/src/ to avoid deployment conflicts. The key thing here is that the value you put in schema.configure must start with exactly the same string you used as the key in mta.yaml. More on this when I get to the errors.

.hdbgrants is the access control piece. Project B's container needs to be explicitly granted a role from Project A. That role needs to already exist over in Project A — it's not something you create in Project B. Your Project A team needs to have a role set up that grants SELECT on the tables you need.

The setup, briefly

On the Cloud Foundry side, you create the UPS once:

cf create-user-provided-service ProjectA_UPS \
  -p '{"schema": "YOUR_PROJECT_A_SCHEMA"}' -t hana

Then in Project B's mta.yaml, you declare it as an existing service resource and bind it to the db-deployer module under SERVICE_REPLACEMENTS:

- name: ProjectA-UPS-resource
  group: SERVICE_REPLACEMENTS
  properties:
    key: ProjectA_UPS
    service: ~{service-name}

Your synonym config then references it like this:

"schema.configure": "ProjectA_UPS/schema"

And once everything deploys, your CAP service code doesn't need to know any of this happened. You just query it:

const db = await cds.connect.to("db");
const results = await db.run(
  `SELECT * FROM "CONSTANTS_TABLE" WHERE "PARAM" = ?`, [value]
);

Clean. No different from querying a local table.

The three errors you will probably hit

I'm not going to pretend this always works first time. Here's what goes wrong most often:

"synonym config refers to unknown service replacement" — This one is almost always a key mismatch. The string you put as key in mta.yaml and the prefix before /schema in your .hdbsynonymconfig need to be identical. Character for character. Check both files side by side.

"insufficient privilege" — The role you referenced in .hdbgrants doesn't exist in Project A, or it exists but doesn't include SELECT on the tables you're trying to reach. You'll need to go back to whoever owns Project A and check what roles are actually deployed there. HANA DB Explorer is your friend here.

"object not found" — The object name in your .hdbsynonym is wrong. The technical name of a table in HDI usually follows a namespace pattern with underscores replacing dots — something like HR_HTR_UTILITY_OBJECTS_CONSTANTS_TABLE. The only reliable way to find the exact name is to look it up directly in HANA DB Explorer under Project A's schema.

One thing worth knowing for local development

When you run cds watch locally, you're using SQLite — there's no HDI container, no synonym. So you need a workaround for local testing.

The simplest approach is to create a CDS entity with @CDS.persistence.exists that mirrors the external table's structure, and seed it with a CSV file. It won't be connected to Project A's live data, but it lets you develop and test the rest of your logic without needing a full BTP deployment every time.

Worth the setup

The first time you wire this up it feels like a lot of moving parts. And honestly, it is. But once it's done, it just works — and it works cleanly. No sync jobs, no API calls for what is essentially a config lookup, no risk of the two sides drifting apart over time.

If your project has shared reference data living in another team's HDI container, this is the right way to access it.

Happy to answer questions in the comments if you hit something I haven't covered here.

#SAP #SAPCAP #SAPHANA #BTP #SAPDevelopment #HDI #CloudFoundry #PwC

entry.title: EXT4 File System Change to XFS File System in SAP HANA Database.
entry.content:
After reading this post,  please check your HANA default File System type. If it is EXT4, please follow this post to change it to XFS.

because HANA does not supported EXT4 File System.

My Operating System is Linux Enterprise Server.

df -hT  command to check File System Type in Linux.

Overview

XFS is designed to give fast performance and handle big data smoothly and It is a high-performance file system used mostly in Linux.  XFS File System is easy to understand  ,  High performance  and  Reads , writes data very fast.

in SAP/HANA environments, XFS is often used for

/hana/data
/hana/log
/hana/shared

 

Below supported filesystems.

GPFS
MPFS*
NFS
OCFS2*
XFS
ext3*
NFS4

Where XFS  is used?

1  Linux servers
2  SAP systems (like HANA environments)
3  Databases
4  Cloud storage
5  Backup systems

 

EXT4 vs XFS Comparison

 

FeatureEXT4XFS
TypeGeneral-purpose file systemHigh-performance file system
Best ForSmall to medium filesLarge files and big data
PerformanceGood for normal workloadsExcellent for heavy workloads
ScalabilityLimited compared to XFSVery high (handles TBs easily)
File SizeSupports large filesSupports very large files efficiently
SpeedGoodFaster for large data operations
JournalingYesYes
RecoveryEasier recovery tools availableRecovery is more complex
UsageOS, apps, general storageDatabases, SAP, large storage systems
Default UseCommon default in LinuxUsed in enterprise environments

 

Team Requirement.

1. SAP Basis Team
Main owner of the activity

Plan downtime and activity steps
Stop & start SAP Application and HANA system
Validate system after change
Coordinate with all teams


2. OS / Linux Team
Responsible for file system change

Create new filesystem (XFS)
Format disks using mkfs.xfs
Mount new file system
Update /etc/fstab
Set correct permissions


3. Backup Team
Ensures data safety

Take full HANA backup before change
Provide restore support if needed

 

Prerequisites.


1. Full System Backup (Mandatory)

Take complete HANA backup (data + log)
Also take OS-level backup if possible.


2. Downtime Planning

File system change requires system downtime
Inform business/users and schedule maintenance window.


3. Check Current File System Type.
df -hT


4. Stop SAP Application and HANA DB Server.
HDB stop
Ensure all HANA services are stopped.

 

Step 1

I am changing here 3 File System Type.

/hana/shared

/hana/data

/hana/log

We can see here ext4 file system showing.

Step 2

Run this command ------zypper install xfsprogs

What is xfsprogs?

xfsprogs  to create, manage, check, and repair XFS file systems on Linux.

xfsprogs has been installed.

Step 3

Take the backup of that File System  which is you want to change.  So i have taken backup of Dir , Files , and all other things which is contain in below File Systems.

/hana/shared

/hana/log

/hana/backup

Step 4

Now Stop the ABAP Application.

Step 5

Now stop Tenant DB from Studio.

ALTER SYSTEM STOP DATABASE SID

 

Step 6 

Now Stop HANA DB

 

Step 7

Check the Process after stop HANA DB

HDB info

HANA DB Stopped

Step 8

Run this command  umount /dev/xvdd    

Target is busy so run this command------à fuser -km /hana/shared

Again, run this command------à      Unmount /dev/xvdd    

Step 9

Now run lsbk command for checking the /hana/shared File System unmount or not.

lsbk

 

We can see in above screenshot xvdd /hana/shared its unmount here.

 

Step 10

Now run this command---  sudo mkfs.xfs -f /dev/xvdd

(for formatting existing partition having different filesystem) its completed.

Step 11

Now change the File System Type. Go to /etc/fstab

Cat /etc/fstab you can see here /hana/shared is ext4

Now open in vi /etc/fstab and change from ext4 to xfs and save :wq!

I red marked under line which is contain ext4 File system now as default.

 

Step 12

Now run this command-----à mount -av its done.

 

Step 13

 

 

Step 14

 

Step 15

Now change fstab file from ext4 to xfs

  Vi /etc/fstab

 

Step 16

mount -av   for checking the mount point

 

Step 17

Copy again all Data in original location and create symbol link.

Cd /usr/sap/HDP

ln -s /hana/shared/HDP/HDB03 /usr/sap/HDP

 

Step 18

After creation link Start the HANA DB.

HDB start

 

 

Step 19

Now start  Tenant DB from Studio.  Tenant DB automatically started when HANA DB started if not started we have to start to manually. 

ALTER SYSTEM START DATABASE H4P

 

Step 20

Now start  Application Server.

Run this command df -hT  to see File system type.

We have xfs File System now.

 

 

 

Conclusion.

Changing from EXT4 to XFS in HANA is recommended to achieve better performance and handle large data efficiently.

Use XFS for HANA systems because it gives better speed and handles large data more efficiently than EXT4.

for Better understanding check this SAP Note 2972496 - SAP HANA Filesystem Types

Please keep this in mind when you are planning to install HANA DB. First check File system Type.

Thanks

entry.title: SSL in HANA Cockpit
entry.content:
SSL Configuration in SAP HANA Cockpit.

SAP HANA Cockpit is a primary administration and recommended tool to manage SAP HANA databases. It provides some additional features and steps that are not available in HANA Studio.

 

Purpose.
This blog describes the steps to configure SSL for the SAP HANA Cockpit.

Overview: SSL Configuration for SAP HANA Cockpit.

Secure Sockets Layer (SSL) configuration for SAP HANA Cockpit is essential to ensure secure and encrypted communication between users (browsers) and the HANA Cockpit system.

 

Key Purpose.

Protect sensitive data from unauthorized access.
Ensure secure login and data transmission.


How It Works.

SSL uses digital certificates to encrypt communication.
The SAP HANA Cockpit is configured to use HTTPS instead of HTTP.
Certificates are issued by a trusted Certificate Authority (CA) or created internally if you have SSO Server in your environment.

 

High-Level Steps.

HANA Cockpit Should be installed in your environment.
Technical Communication User should be created.
SYSTEM and TENANT DB must be added in Cockpit.
Generate SSL certificate from OS level with sidadm user.
Sign certificate with your CA authority or you can sign self if SSO Server in your environment.
Import certificate into HANA XSA keystore.
Configure HTTPS settings in HANA Cockpit.
Restart Cockpit services to activate SSL.
Access Cockpit using https://:

Benefits.
Secure browser access.

Prerequisites.

HANA Cockpit Should be installed in your environment.
Sign certificate with your CA authority.
SSO Server installed (Optional)

Team Requirements.

Basis Consultant will install Cockpit and Configure SSL.

Certificate Authority  will Sign Certificate. 

Now We are going to Configure SSL in HANA Cockpit.

Step 1

Launch HANA Cockpit. We can see in Green Color Not secure coming in HANA Cockpit URL.

Step 2

Run env for checking the sec dir path

Go to this path ---> /usr/sap/D1C/HDB00/ and take backup of sec dir.

Step 3

Backup has been taken with sec_bkp_21_05_2021 name

 

Step 4

Now generate the pse and csr name. I am generating here with this name domain.pse and domain.csr

Now run below command.


sapgenpse gen_pse -p domain.pse -.r domain.csr -k "GN-dNSName:FQDN.com" -k "GN-dNSName:hostname" "CN=FQDN.com, OU=IT,

Step 5

Now check ls –ltr and see domanin.pse and domain.csr file generated.  in /usr/sap/dic/HDB00/hostname/sec

Step 6

Now open domain.csr file for checking the certificate.  Copy this Certificate and Sign it from your CA Authority or SSO Server.

We have our SSO Server for signing the certificate.

I am login in my SSO Server.

Now login in SSO Server.

Step 7

Go to Certificate Management--> Sign certificate requests and paste that certificate.

Click on Show Certificate Request.

Step 8

Click on Sign Certificate and after Sign copy the Certificate

Step 9

 

Step 10

Now create one file with domain.p7b and paste Signed certificate in this file.

/usr/sap/SID/HDB00/Hostname/sec

Step 11

I pasted Singed Certificated in domain.p7b file.

 

Step 12

Now import the certificate with below command.

sapgenpse import_own_cert -p domain.pse -c domain.p7b

Certificated imported Successfully.

Step 13

Now we need to set certificate with domain. What domain we have run xs domains command for checking the domain name. We have only one domain.

 

Before set the certificate run this command for checking the certificate status. xs domain-certificates

We have no certificate now.

 

Step 14

Now run this for set the certificate    xs set-certificate host --pse domain.pse

 

Step 15

Certificate done now restart the XSA from XSA restart command.

Step 16

Now check again XSA Domain Certificate   xs domain-certificates we can see certificate with signed.

Now check the https URL from xs version command.

You can see hana-cockpit URL showing https://hostname:51026

Step 17

Now run this command XSA diagnose for checking the issue in system.

Now issue coming all is ok.

Step 18

Now run this command for open the Cockpit with ----> https://hostname:51026

 

HANA Cockpit SSL has been completed.

 

Conclusion

SSL in SAP HANA Cockpit makes the system safe by protecting data between the user and the system.
It allows access using HTTPS, which improves security and reliability.
