entry.title: Building Custom AI Agents with SAP Joule Studio: A Practical Guide Part-1
entry.content:
Introduction

Joule is already available in SAP Launchpad for many users, and it is great for broad questions.
My challenge started when business users asked for operational actions, not guidance.

A user asked:
“Show all blocked sales orders for customer 17100001 and remove the delivery block for order 487.”

I am unable to directly retrieve or display information on open sales orders due to system limitations. However, I can guide you on how to use the 'Sales Order - Query' to access this information manually. Would you like me to provide instructions on how to do this?

Out of the box, Joule gave a helpful explanation, but it did not execute the business action.
So I built a custom agent in Joule Studio that could read and update sales orders in SAP S/4HANA through OData APIs.

The Two Building Blocks: Skills & Agents

Everything in Joule Studio revolves around two core concepts:

1. Skill — A Specific Task

A  skill is a single, focused capability. Think of it as one action your agent can perform. It could be:

  1. A CRUD operation (Create, Read, Update, Delete)
  2. A process or automation
  3. A  condition check
  4. Launching another Joule skill
  5. Each skill does one thing well. For example:
    • Read all sales orders
    • Update delivery block on a sales order
    • Create a new sales order
    • Delete sales order.
2. Agent — The Orchestrator

An agent is the brain that holds multiple skills together. It decides when to use which skill based on what the user asks. An agent can even call other agents — making it possible to build complex, multi-step workflows.

Anatomy of an Agent
When you create an agent in Joule Studio, you'll work with several important configuration areas:

Expertise & Instructions

  1. Expertise — Tell the agent what domain it specializes in. Is it for HR? Sales? Procurement? This helps the agent understand its boundaries.
  2. Instructions — This is where the magic happens. You're essentially writing a playbook for the agent:
    1. What to do and what not to do
    2. How to format responses
    3. When to trigger which skill
    4. Rules and guardrail

Additional Context — Common abbreviations, company-specific terminology, or any contextual information the agent might need to understand your business language.

Model Settings
You get to choose your LLM provider:

  • Anthropic
  • OpenAI
  • Google
  • MistralAI

Enable Backup LLM Provider. This sets up automatic failover — if your primary provider is slow or unavailable, the backup kicks in automatically, keeping your agent running without interruption.

MCP Servers
Add MCP (Model Context Protocol) Servers to give your agent access to additional tools and external capabilities.

Tools
Add tools that provide the agent with additional context, system data access, or the ability to perform actions.

  • calculator
  • joule skill
  • joule agent
  • Documents(Document Grounding)

Agent Output

Define how the output should be formatted:

  1. Markdown
  2. Text
  3. Data Type
  4. JSON
What I Built (Actual Implementation)

I implemented a Sales Order Assistant agent for a finance and sales operations scenario.

Scope of the agent:

  • Read sales orders with filters (customer, status, billing date, block flags)
  • Update delivery block on a specific sales order
  • Return a table-style summary for business users
  • Refuse to answer if live data cannot be read

Technical stack:

  • Joule Studio agent
  • 3 custom skills (GET, PATCH, POST)
  • SAP S/4HANA OData service: API_SALES_ORDER_SRV
  • Destination-based connectivity in test environment
  • Primary + backup LLM provider enabled
Step 1: Skills I Created

I separated skills by operation so tool selection remained predictable.

1. Read Sales Orders (GET)
Purpose: Retrieve sales orders and basic status fields.

2. Modify Sales Order (PATCH)
Purpose: Update delivery block and selected editable fields.

3. Create Sales Order (POST)
Purpose: Create a new order for approved scenarios.

Why this helped:
When I initially combined read and update into one “do everything” skill, the agent often chose the wrong operation. Splitting skills reduced tool-calling errors significantly.

Step 2: Agent Instructions I Actually Used

The single biggest improvement came from strict instructions.

Key rules I configured:

1. Always call Read Sales Orders before any status answer.
2. Call Modify Sales Order only when user intent is clearly an update.
3. Never generate order numbers, dates, or statuses without API output.
4. On 4XX/5XX, return a clear business-safe error message and next step.

Response format:

  • Always output a compact table for list queries.
  • Always include order ID, customer, billing date, and status.
  • Flag risky records: overdue billing, missing assignment, missing reference.

What changed after this:
Before strict rules, I saw occasional “plausible” but unverified responses. After adding mandatory tool-use rules, those stopped.

Step 3: Real Test Cases and Results

I tested using business-like prompts.

Test prompts:

  • “Show all blocked sales orders for customer 17100001.”
  • “Remove delivery block on order 500001234.”
  • “List overdue orders with missing assignment.”

Observed behavior:

  • Read queries: agent called GET skill, then summarized correctly.
  • Update queries: agent called PATCH skill with target order.
  • Failure case (invalid order): agent returned API error context and suggested verification steps.

One practical lesson:
If the API payload is too large, response quality drops. I reduced returned fields to only what users need and got more stable answers.

These were the most useful implementation learnings :

1. Keep skills narrow and explicit
Broad “multi-action” skills increase wrong tool selection.

2. Put tool-calling rules at the top of instructions
Priority placement improved consistency.

3. Add business-safe refusal behavior
If data retrieval fails, the agent must refuse to guess.

4. Minimize API response payload early
Too many fields create noisy reasoning and weaker summaries.

5. Add backup LLM provider, but validate output style
Failover worked, but I had to align formatting instructions across models.

6. Test with ambiguous prompts
Examples like “fix this order” forced me to add clarification logic before updates.

Execution Trace: What Happens Under the Hood (From My Test Runs)
For a prompt like “Show blocked orders for customer 17100001”:

1. Agent starts and loads instructions/context
2. Intent classified as data retrieval
3. GET skill called with filters
4. Raw response compressed
5. Agent applies formatting + risk flags
6. Final response returned as table

For an update prompt:

1. Agent detects action intent
2. Validates target order context via read
3. PATCH skill executes update
4. Agent confirms changed field and status

Real-World Example: Sales Order Assistant
Let me walk you through a practical example. My requirement was to build an agent that can:

  1. Handle natural language queries like *"Show all open orders for customer X"* or *"Remove delivery block on order Y"*
  2. Provide contextual insights (customer credit status, expected delivery dates)
  3. Suggest corrective actions and guide users through SAP Fiori apps

Step 1: Pick Your API

I went to the SAP Business Accelerator Hub and chose the standard OData service: `API_SALES_ORDER_SRV/`

Step 2: Create Skills

I built three separate skills — one for each operation:

Skill Operation Purpose
Read Sales OrdersGET Fetch all sales orders
Update Sales OrderPATCH Modify fields like delivery block
Create Sales OrderPOSTCreate a new sales order


The GET skill is that it retrieves all sales orders, but the agent processes the response based on your question. Ask for a specific customer? It filters and gives you only that data — not the entire list.

Step 3: Configure the Agent

Here's an example of how I set up the expertise and instructions:

Expertise:

You are an SAP Sales Order Assistant. Your expertise includes:
- Retrieving sales order details from SAP S/4HANA using OData services
- Explaining order status, delivery dates, and pricing information
- Helping users understand delivery blocks and billing workflows

Instructions:
CRITICAL RULES:
1. You MUST ALWAYS call the "Read Sales Order" skill before answering
ANY question about sales orders. NEVER answer from your own knowledge.
2. You MUST call "Modify Sales Order" skill when user tries to modify
a sales order.
3. If you cannot call the tool, inform the user that you need to
retrieve the data and cannot answer without it.

RESPONSIBILITIES:
- Retrieve sales order headers (order number, customer, billing date, status)
- Summarize order volumes by customer group, date range, or status
- Identify orders requiring attention (overdue billing, missing assignments)
- Present results in a clear table format

RESPONSE FORMAT:
| # | Sales Order | Customer Group | Billing Date | Status |
|---|------------|----------------|--------------|--------|
| 1 | 4500001234 | Group 1 | 2026-05-25 | Open |

DO NOT:
- Guess or fabricate order data
- Ignore Error 4XX responses — always inform the user

 

This is a basic example — in a real scenario, you'd provide far more detailed instructions based on your specific business requirements.

Additional Context

FIELD DEFINITIONS:
- Reference → Order reference / external document number
- Customer Group 1-4 → Classification groupings for the customer
- Assignment → Cost assignment or project reference
- Billing Date → Planned billing/invoicing date
- Exchng. Rate Accntg. → Exchange rate used for accounting
- Addit. Value Date → Additional value date for payment terms

SORTING & PRIORITY RULES:
- Default sort: Billing Date (earliest first)
- Highlight orders where Billing Date < today (overdue)
- Flag orders with empty Assignment or Reference fields

ERROR HANDLING:
- If API returns Error 4XX → inform user, suggest checking
authorization or filters

ORDER STATUS LOGIC:
- Billing Date < today + no invoice = "Overdue"
- Assignment field empty = "Needs Attention"
- Reference field empty = "Missing Reference"

Step 4: Connect Skills to Agent

The connection is straightforward:

Agent → Tools → Add Skill → Select your skill

Do this for each skill. Then, in your instructions, specify when the agent should trigger which skill.

Step 5: Test

Click Test, select your destination for the action calls, select your private environment, and start chatting:

  1. Give me sales order 987
  2. Show me all blocked sales orders
  3. Remove the delivery block on order 456

What Happens Under the Hood?

When you test your agent, you can see the internal execution trace. Here's what happens when a user asks something:

User Message

 

Breaking It Down:

PhaseWhat It Does
StartInitializes the agent, loads context, expertise, and instructions
Direct PromptingChecks if the request can be answered without complex reasoning
Decomposition Breaks the request into smaller sub-tasks
Plan Creates an execution plan — decides order of steps, which APIs to call
Agent Thinking The LLM reasons about the plan and decides the action to take
ReadExecutes the API call to S/4HANA
CompressorSummarizes the raw API response to stay within token limits
Agent Thinking (2nd)Processes compressed data and formulates the answer
Final Answer

Produces the user-facing response

Refinement

Quality check for accuracy, formatting, and completeness

End

Execution completes

Conclusion

The future of enterprise AI isn't just about having an AI assistant. It's about having your AI assistant, built for your business.

Joule gives strong baseline assistance, but business operations need domain-specific actions.
Joule Studio is where that becomes practical: you define the tools, the guardrails, and the behavior.

My key takeaway after implementation:
Good agent quality is less about which model and more about:

  • strict tool-calling instructions,
  • small and clean payloads,
  • and real-world test prompts with edge cases.

If you are building your first Joule Studio agent, start with one business process, 2-3 narrow skills, and strict non-hallucination rules. You will get better results faster than trying to build a broad agent on day one.

Remember: you're essentially teaching a new team member how to do their job. The clearer your instructions, the better the results. Once everything is working as expected — launch it, and let your custom agent work for your users.

entry.title: Reporting of GHG emissions - 2 options of receiving actual emissions sent by your carrier
entry.content:
Good news – we have successfully implemented and tested the scope defined in our Roadmap for Transportation Management in SAP S/4HANA 2025 Feature Pack Stack 2

Subject to the overall availability of SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS2, users can look forward to further improvements in the Sustainability area, alongside other new innovations outlined in our Roadmap, starting in October 26 (*).

(*) Subject to change

This blog is the third in a sequence of three posts. It provides additional information for those interested in Transportation Management and Sustainability. It serves as supplementary material to the initial blog Reporting of GHG Emissions. We recommend reading that post first to understand the basics.

TM in SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS2 further contributes to Sustainability by enhancing the Service Interface TransportationEventBulkNotification_In with two new fields—one for the emission value and another for the unit of measure—and its underlying backend. This mirrors the approach outlined in the initial blog and offers a more flexible reporting solution. As promised, we continue to support your sustainability journey.

In this follow-up blog post, we aim to provide you with the necessary knowledge to enable receiving actual emission values at both the document level and the stage level.

Following this approach, an inbound message sent externally—for instance, by the carrier—will include two new fields: one for the emission value and another for the unit of measure.

 

Option 1 - reporting on stage level

Stage 1 might be the one starting at Stop 10 and ending at Stop 20.

Hence, the reporting on stage 1 uses EventTypeCode 'REPORT_EMIVAL_STAGE' for Stop 20.

Example FO:  6100018959

Use the following example XML as a sample in order to update the freight order with reported CO2 emissions on stage level.

Fig. 1 illustrates the enhanced payload for reporting on stage level.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Option 2 - reporting on document level

The reporting on document level uses EventTypeCode 'REPORT_EMIVAL_DOC'.

Example FO:  6100018941

Use the following example XML as a sample in order to update the freight order with reported CO2 emissions on header level.

Fig. 2 illustrates the enhanced payload for reporting on document level.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Let us assume our FO consists of 2 stages. The distances involved might be the following:

Stage 1: 478 km; Stage 2: 279 km; Ratio: 1.71

Fig. 3 illustrates the stages and their distances.The carrier reports 3001 KG on document level. Activating the views for the carbon emissions in the Freight Order will display a window showing the reported carbon emissions.

Fig. 4 illustrates the reported carbon emissions.The emissions reported at the header level are arithmetically distributed across the stage levels (Stage 1: 1,895 kg; Stage 2: 1,106 kg; ratio: 1.71), taking stage distances into account for illustration purposes only. The actual emissions data is stored and recorded at the document level in the database.

That's it for now.

Stay tuned, there is even more to come.

entry.title: reCAP 2026 – Recordings of our Annual CAP Developer Conference
entry.content:
On 15 July 2026, the CAP community came together for reCAP 2026 in St. Leon-Rot. Again, tickets were sold out within just a few minutes. We had more than 400 participants onsite and many more joining online.

For everyone who couldn’t attend live, most sessions were recorded. Below is a summary of the recorded sessions, along with links to the recordings. You can find all recordings on YouTube.


 

Opening & Keynote

Intro by Ole Lilienthal
Setting the stage, Ole gave an overview of the Autonomous Enterprise and how CAP plays a key role in enabling the SAP Business AI platform as the underlying platform for the Autonomous Enterprise. He also explained the importance of CAP in the age of AI.  

Recording link

Keynote by Daniel Hutzel
In his keynote, Daniel shared an overview of the biggest CAP achievements since the last reCAP, including AI-related topics.

Recording link

 

Main Track Sessions in Audimax

CAP Tools – What’s New and Hot by Christian Georgi
A tour through the latest and greatest in CAP tooling, including:

  • `cds repl` with queries
  • Debugging apps on Kyma
  • CDS Project outline in Visual Studio Code
  • CAP in browser

Recording link

Optimizing CAP Performance with Data Replication and Caching – Part II by Mike Zaschka
As a follow-up to last year’s talk on cds-caching, this session broadened the view to the full landscape of caching, replication, and federation strategies in the CAP ecosystem. Mike walked through a curated selection of solutions and offered practical guidance on which approach fits which scenario.

Recording link

AI-Assisted Development of CAP Apps by Daniel Schlachter and Stefan Rudi
A look at the CAP MCP development server, which gives AI coding assistants direct access to CAP documentation improving code quality in tools like Claude Code and GitHub Copilot.

Recording link

Expressions and Abstractions – Two Developer Superpowers in CAP by Patrice Bender and DJ Adams
A demonstration of two powerful CAP affordances: expressions (what they are, how to use them, and why they matter) and mocking (letting you iterate fast on data, auth, messaging, and remote services).

Recording link

CAP Plugins: Latest and Greatest by Max Eckert, Dries Van Vaerenbergh, Matthis Vansteenhuyse, and Lisa Julia Nebel
CAP plugins dramatically simplify integration with SAP BTP services. This session demystified the plugin model and spotlighted the enhanced attachments plugin, the renovated change tracking plugin, a new notifications plugin for Java, and the new SAP Build Process Automation plugin.

Recording link

Plug, Connect, Play: CAP Integration Made Effortless by Ram Prasad GS and Bilal El-Massoudi
An introduction to cap-connect, a production-ready, open-source CAP plugin from Aarini Consulting that brings first-class connection management to any CAP application. The talk also showcased how cap-connect powers Prodsphere, Aarini’s own CAP-based product.

Recording link

Production GenAI in CAP: Beyond the Demo by Moudhaffer Azizi
This session explored the architecture behind a production-grade CAP application on SAP BTP that uses RAG to analyse custom ABAP code against Clean Core standards. The session highlighted the journey from a single prompt to a resilient solution orchestrating 14 AI-powered operations.

Recording link

Beyond Vibe Coding: Engineering Deterministic CAP Applications with SDD by Maximilian Hartig and Abdulbasıt Gülşen
A live "0-to-1" walkthrough of Spec-Driven Development using the open-source Spec Kit in Visual Studio Code to transform a raw business requirement into a CAP application.

Recording link

From Click-Ops to Code-Ops (Terraform) by Nicholas Arefta
Nicholas demonstrated how to use Terraform to deploy multitenant apps on SAP BTP. He demonstrated provisioning of a Provider subaccount, deploying a multi-tenant application and onboarding a Subscriber completely hands-free.

Recording link

Custom Business Logic with Predefined Extension Points – Using CDS-Oyster by Nick Josipovic and Konrad Koschel
Together with Subscription Billing and Cloud Foundation, Nick and Konrad built an extensive Extension Box using CAP and the CDS-Oyster Code Sandbox enabling secure code extensions in a complex cloud environment.

Recording link

Implementing Runtime Plugins in CAP Java by Joshua Mitchell
Joshua demonstrated in his live session how to program a basic soft delete plugin for CAP Java using annotations, the IntelliJ debugger, and an expression evaluator.

Recording link

 

Side Track Sessions in W1/W2

Building Code-Based AI Agents with CAP and UI5: From Concept to Production by Wouter Lemaire

Using the Smart Monitoring Agent (previously demonstrated at SAP TechEd) as a real-world example, Wouter showed how to build intelligent, code-based AI agents by combining SAP AI Core with CAP and UI5. The session covered the complete journey from development to deployment.

Recording link

Building AI-enabled Applications with CAP by Max Eckert, Simon Engel, Markus Riedinger, and Peter W. Szabo

An overview of how CAP is evolving to meet the AI era including: exposing CAP services as endpoints for AI agents, leveraging the CAP AI plugin, exploring additional features, and building intelligent, code-driven agents.

Recording link

Building a CAP Application with Latest Best Practices and AI by Lukas Theis, Johannes Vogt, Marc Becker, and Robin de Silva Jayasinghe

A practical, end-to-end walkthrough from idea to production-ready CAP app, combining proven CAP architecture patterns with AI-assisted workflows.

Recording link

Declarative Programming Patterns in CAP - Less Code, More Power by Adrian Görler, Robin de Silva Jayasinghe, and Vitaly Kozyura

A look at how declarative mechanisms in CAP such as calculated elements, annotation-based constraints (e.g. @assert, @assert.range, @assert.format), and field control help to reduce custom code. The session also covered how status transition flows help reducing the need for custom validation logic while ensuring data integrity.

Recording link

Update from the cap-js-community - Experience the latest additions to CAP by Oliver Klemenz

A focused technical update on the latest enhancements in the cap-js-community, with a deep dive into the WebSocket modules and the newly introduced common utilities module.

Recording link

AI-Assisted Security Hardening of CAP Applications by Matthias Braun

Matthias showed how AI-powered tools can help identify authorization gaps, generate comprehensive security tests, and detect attack vectors. This significantly reduces the effort required to build and maintain secure CAP applications.

Recording link

Event Queues Scheduling by Sebastian Van Syckel, Dietrich Mostowoj, Thomas Bonk, and Lars Plessing
A deep dive into Transactional Event Queues, which allow scheduling of events and background tasks for asynchronous, exactly-once processing with strong resilience.

Recording link

Automated Testing with HANA Cloud by Armin Hatting
Armin presented practical approaches to integrating database-supported testing in CAP applications, setting up realistic test environments, and ensuring the functionality of SAP HANA Cloud native logic.

Recording link

Did you know about infix filters and projection functions? by Nico Schoenteich

A live-coding overview of infix filters and projection functions (Node.js) and how they were applied in a recent project to reduce custom service.

Recording link

 

Thank You

A huge thank-you to all speakers, attendees, and community members who made reCAP 2026 such an inspiring day. 🙌 The energy, the ideas, and the conversations are exactly what makes the CAP community special.

If you missed any session, use the recording links above to catch up.

We look forward to seeing how you use what you learned at reCAP 2026. If you have any feedback, please let us know.

Happy coding! 😊

entry.title: Integrating Amazon FBA with SAP S4 HANA - Part 3
entry.content:
Just in case you landed here directly, this is the third chapter in this series about integrating SAP S/4HANA with Amazon Seller Central. This article focuses on Amazon FBA SAP S4 HANA integration.

Unlike FBM, Amazon FBA is best modelled as a consignment process because Amazon physically holds and ships the inventory while the merchant continues to own it until the product is sold.

  • In the first part I covered Amazon merchant models, the Selling Partner API, master data setup, and the process of obtaining an Amazon Developer ID. Here is the link to the article How to Get Started with SAP S/4HANA and Amazon Seller Central Integration 
  • In the second part on Fulfilled by Merchant, I explained how Amazon FBM maps to the standard SAP Sales and Distribution order-to-cash cycle.

That distinction changes how inventory, revenue, receivables, tax, returns, reimbursements, and Amazon settlements should be recorded in SAP S/4HANA.

 

US division of a European Food Processing Company closed down their Amazon marketplace simply because they weren't able to manage the processes and reconcile their financials.  
A nutrition supplement company outsources their complete Amazon operations for a significant fees to a third party provider.

If you Amazon FBA is the model you are exploring for your business, this tutorial will help simplify the entire concept and give you some confidence 😊 of mapping the process in the SAP terminologies. 

Amazon FBA SAP S4 HANA Integration: Complete Consignment Process

Amazon Fulfilled by Amazon, commonly called FBA, allows merchants to send inventory in bulk to Amazon fulfillment centers. Amazon then stores the inventory and manages order picking, packing, customer delivery, customer service, and returns.

In SAP S/4HANA, this model closely matches the customer consignment process. The goods remain the merchant’s property while they are stored at Amazon. Revenue is recognized only when a customer purchases the product and Amazon fulfills the order.

Amazon FBA and SAP S/4HANA: Key Takeaways

Here are the key takeaways from mapping Amazon FBA with Consignment Process within SAP. 

  • Stock sent to Amazon is recorded as customer consignment stock.
  • Inventory remains owned by the merchant until the customer sale.
  • No revenue is recognized when goods are initially sent to Amazon.
  • A customer purchase creates a consignment issue in SAP.
  • Customer returns create consignment returns and credit adjustments.
  • Unsold stock returned by Amazon creates a consignment pick-up.
  • Amazon settlements must be matched against sales, fees, returns, reserves, and reimbursements.
  • Special stock indicator W keeps Amazon-held inventory separate and visible.

What Is Amazon FBA? What are the steps involved business process wise

Business process wise Amazon FBA cycle starts with the following steps.

  • Identifying merchandize you will be selling - A big decision is which of your products you would want to launch on Amazon FBA store. Of course these are subsets of products that have a B2C demand and some market research to back it up. 
  • Sending a bulk shipment to Amazon Warehouse / Distribution Center - The first shipment that you sent to Amazon Warehouse, it often a butterfly in the stomach instance. You need to create a shipment prior to this with all the items that you intend to send to Amazon. You may also do some carrier shopping. In many cases I have seen Amazon's own carrier being the most cost effective.

 

  • When Amazon order comes through - As customers place orders, Amazon fulfills them and sends the information back to SAP. Each order is recorded as a consignment issue. In SAP, Amazon remains both the sold-to party and the payer, while the ship-to address is the end customer. Having the correct ship-to address is critical because it determines the tax calculation. The tax shown in SAP should match what Amazon collects and pays on behalf of the seller. Since pricing is driven by Amazon, you will need manual conditions within SAP to sync the pricing components to match Amazon will pay you at the period end. 

When Amazon Order comes to SAP, you need to ensure that you map correct ASIN number into customer material Number and the Net Line item and Order amount should match what Amazon will pay you in their settlement cycle for this order. 

Having synchronization at periodic intervals, keeps your A/R accurate. 

  • Returns are also tracked. When Amazon processes a return, SAP creates a consignment return, followed by a return delivery and credit memo. This keeps your accounts receivable aligned with Amazon’s adjustments.
  • Finally, Payment reconciliation takes place. Every two weeks, Amazon sends a bulk payment that covers all orders, less fees, reserves, or special charges. With proper integration, the invoices in SAP match Amazon’s payouts, reducing manual work. Although there may still be small adjustments for account-level charges, most of the process runs automatically, saving time and effort for the finance team.

Under Amazon FBA, merchants do not ship each customer order themselves. Instead, they create an inbound shipment and send inventory in bulk to Amazon’s fulfillment network.

Amazon then handles inventory storage, order picking, packaging, delivery to the customer, customer service, customer returns, as well as selected inventory adjustments and reimbursements. This allows sellers to outsource most fulfillment operations while Amazon manages the end-to-end logistics and post-purchase experience.

The merchant continues to manage product listings, pricing, procurement, accounting, and inventory ownership.

Why Merchants Choose Amazon FBA

Merchants choose Amazon FBA because it reduces logistics work and gives customers access to Amazon’s fulfillment network.

1. Lower Logistics Overhead

Merchants no longer need to operate their own pick-pack-ship process for FBA products. This can reduce warehouse workload, packaging effort, and carrier management.

2. Faster Customer Delivery

Amazon distributes inventory across its fulfillment network and places products closer to buyers. This can shorten delivery estimates and improve the customer experience.

3. Prime Eligibility and Buyer Trust

FBA products may qualify for Prime delivery. Customers also receive Amazon-managed tracking, support, and returns, which can increase confidence in the purchase.

4. Lower Exposure to Carrier Delays

In an FBM model, the merchant is responsible for carrier selection and delivery performance. Late deliveries can affect the merchant’s Amazon performance metrics.

Under FBA, Amazon assumes much of this fulfillment responsibility.

5. Greater Sales Potential

Fast delivery, Prime eligibility, and Amazon-backed customer service can make an FBA listing more attractive than a comparable merchant-fulfilled listing.

The trade-off is that Amazon gains physical control of the inventory while the merchant continues to own it. This is the main reason FBA should be treated as a consignment process in SAP.

Why Amazon FBA Is a Consignment Process in SAP

When products are stored inside an Amazon fulfillment center, they remain the merchant’s inventory.

Amazon has physical custody of the goods, but ownership has not transferred. No customer sale has occurred, and revenue should not be recognised.

This is the standard definition of consignment stock: goods stored at a customer or partner location that remain the seller’s property until they are consumed or sold.

SAP S/4HANA provides a four-step consignment cycle that maps closely to Amazon FBA.

What Is Special Stock Indicator W?

Special stock indicator W identifies customer consignment stock in SAP.

It allows Amazon-held inventory to remain visible and valued as an asset while keeping it separate from normal unrestricted stock in the merchant’s warehouse.

This distinction is important for inventory reporting, balance-sheet accuracy, available-to-promise calculations, and reconciliation with Amazon’s inventory reports.

Amazon FBA Integration Architecture

A complete Amazon FBA SAP S4 HANA integration normally connects Amazon Seller Central, Amazon Selling Partner API, SAP Sales and Distribution, Materials Management, Finance, and an integration platform or middleware layer.

The integration must support bidirectional data movement.

Data Moving from SAP to Amazon

  • Product and SKU data
  • Inventory planned for inbound shipments
  • Shipment and carton details
  • Product identifiers and mappings
  • Selected pricing or availability information

Data Moving from Amazon to SAP

  • FBA customer orders
  • Shipment and fulfillment data
  • Customer delivery addresses
  • Returns and refunds
  • FBA inventory balances
  • Lost and damaged inventory adjustments
  • Amazon fees and commissions
  • Reimbursements
  • Settlement and payout reports

Master Data Required for Amazon FBA SAP S4 HANA Integration

Reliable master data is the foundation of the integration. Incorrect mappings will eventually create inventory differences, tax errors, unmatched settlements, and incorrect receivables.

Amazon as Customer or Business Partner

Amazon must be created as a customer or business partner because the SAP consignment cycle is executed against a customer account.

Figure - Create Amazon as a business partner against which the consignment cycle will execute. 

In a common FBA design:

  • Sold-To Party: Amazon
  • Payer: Amazon
  • Ship-To: The end customer’s delivery address

Amazon is responsible for settling the merchant’s proceeds, but the end customer’s address is required for tax calculation and transaction reporting.

Amazon Consignment Inventory Structure

The merchant must decide how Amazon-held inventory will be represented in SAP.

Depending on the implementation, FBA stock may be organised through a dedicated plant, storage location, customer consignment structure, or reporting dimension.

Many businesses maintain a dedicated storage location or reporting structure so Amazon inventory is never confused with stock physically available in their own warehouse.

ASIN, Seller SKU, and SAP Material Mapping

Every Amazon ASIN or Seller SKU must map to the correct SAP material number.

This mapping may be maintained through Customer Material Information Records or an integration-layer mapping table.

The mapping should work in both directions:

  • Amazon ASIN or Seller SKU to SAP material number
  • SAP material number to Amazon ASIN or Seller SKU

This mapping is required for fill-ups, customer sales, returns, inventory adjustments, reimbursements, and settlement reconciliation.

Amazon Fee and SAP Condition-Type Mapping

Amazon commissions, FBA fulfillment fees, discounts, promotions, and other order-level charges should be mapped to appropriate SAP pricing condition types.

This allows the SAP net receivable to align with the order-level amount Amazon reports as payable.

 

Consignment Fill-Up: Sending Inventory to Amazon

The FBA process begins when the merchant sends inventory to Amazon.

This step involves coordinated activity in Amazon Seller Central and SAP S/4HANA.

Amazon-Side Inbound Shipment Process

In Seller Central, the merchant creates an inbound shipment or FBA shipment plan.

The merchant provides:

  • Products being sent
  • Quantity of each product
  • Source warehouse
  • Carton configuration
  • Preparation details
  • Shipping method

Amazon provides item labels, carton labels, preparation instructions, and fulfillment-center destinations.

Amazon may divide one inbound shipment across several fulfillment centers based on its inventory-placement requirements.

SAP Consignment Fill-Up Process

In SAP S/4HANA, the corresponding process normally includes:

  1. Creation of a consignment fill-up sales order
  2. Creation of an outbound delivery
  3. Picking and packing
  4. Post Goods Issue

The goods issue moves stock from the merchant’s warehouse into Amazon customer consignment stock under special stock indicator W.

No Revenue at Consignment Fill-Up

No revenue or customer receivable should be posted when inventory is sent to Amazon.

The merchant has moved inventory to another physical location, but the inventory has not yet been sold.

Recognising revenue during the fill-up would overstate sales, receivables, profit, and potentially tax.

Tracking Multiple Amazon Fulfillment Centers

Amazon may send an inbound shipment to several fulfillment centers and later redistribute units within its network.

SAP does not always need to represent every fulfillment center as a separate accounting location. Many merchants track the aggregate consignment quantity held by Amazon while allowing Amazon to manage fulfillment-center-level placement.

Businesses requiring detailed operational reporting can maintain additional fulfillment-center visibility in the integration layer.

Consignment Issue: Processing Amazon FBA Customer Orders

When a customer buys a product, Amazon picks, packs, and ships it from an FBA fulfillment center.

Amazon then makes the order and fulfillment information available through SP-API endpoints and reports.

In SAP, the completed customer sale is recorded as a consignment issue.

This is the moment when:

  • The product leaves special stock W.
  • Ownership transfers to the buyer.
  • Revenue is recognised.
  • The Amazon receivable is posted.
  • Relevant fees and taxes are recorded.

Amazon Order Number

The Amazon order number should be stored in a searchable SAP reference field. Many implementations map it to the customer purchase-order field.

This reference is required for customer service, returns, settlement matching, and audit trails.

Sold-To, Payer, and Ship-To Determination

Amazon normally remains the Sold-To Party and Payer because Amazon settles the merchant’s proceeds.

The end customer’s address is used as the Ship-To information. This address is especially important for tax determination.

Product and Quantity Mapping

Each Amazon SKU or ASIN must resolve to the correct SAP material. The consignment issue must consume the correct quantity from Amazon consignment stock.

Amazon Fees and Net Receivables

Amazon commissions, FBA fulfillment fees, discounts, and other order-level charges should be represented through SAP pricing condition types or related financial postings.

A useful validation rule is:

If the SAP net receivable does not match Amazon’s order-level payable amount, the fee or pricing mapping is incomplete.

Amazon Marketplace Facilitator Tax in SAP

Tax requires special attention in an Amazon FBA integration.

Under Marketplace Facilitator laws in the United States and similar rules in other regions, Amazon may calculate, collect, and remit sales tax on behalf of the merchant.

Why the Ship-To Address Matters

Tax generally follows the customer’s delivery destination. The end customer’s address must therefore be available in SAP even when Amazon is the Sold-To Party and Payer.

The ship-to address allows SAP to determine the correct tax jurisdiction and compare the result with Amazon’s tax reporting.

Statistical Tax Condition

Amazon-collected tax is often represented as a statistical condition in SAP.

This keeps the amount visible and reconcilable without incorrectly treating it as tax the merchant must remit where Amazon is responsible for remittance.

The SAP tax amount should align with Amazon’s Marketplace Facilitator reporting. A close match indicates that the ship-to and tax determination logic is working correctly.

Consignment Return: Processing Amazon Customer Returns

When Amazon accepts a customer return and places the product back into sellable FBA inventory, the transaction should create a consignment return in SAP.

The SAP process normally includes:

  1. Consignment return order
  2. Return delivery
  3. Inventory movement back into special stock W
  4. Credit memo linked to the original transaction

The return should reference the original Amazon order and the original SAP consignment issue whenever possible.

This ensures that SAP:

  • Reduces or reverses the original receivable
  • Restores eligible inventory to Amazon consignment stock
  • Records the credit memo correctly
  • Matches Amazon’s customer refund

Sellable and Unsellable FBA Returns

Not every customer return is placed back into sellable inventory.

Amazon may classify returned inventory as:

  • Sellable
  • Customer damaged
  • Carrier damaged
  • Warehouse damaged
  • Defective
  • Unsellable

The integration should use this disposition to determine whether the unit returns to sellable consignment stock or requires an adjustment, reimbursement claim, disposal, or removal order.

Consignment Pick-Up: Returning Unsold Inventory

Slow-moving FBA inventory can generate storage and aged-inventory charges. Merchants may also want to discontinue a product, change packaging, or move stock to another sales channel.

In these situations, the merchant creates a removal order in Seller Central.

When Amazon returns the inventory, SAP processes a consignment pick-up.

The inventory movement is:

Amazon consignment stock under special stock W → merchant-owned warehouse inventory.

This closes the inventory loop for goods sent to Amazon but never sold.

Amazon FBA Inventory Reconciliation in SAP

Inventory reconciliation is one of the most important differences between Amazon FBA and FBM.

Under FBM, the merchant physically controls the inventory. Under FBA, Amazon stores, transfers, fulfills, and adjusts the inventory across its network.

FBA units can become:

  • Lost inside the fulfillment network
  • Damaged by Amazon
  • Damaged by a carrier
  • Disposed of
  • Transferred between fulfillment centers
  • Reserved for customer orders
  • Classified as unsellable
  • Reimbursed by Amazon

SAP consignment stock should be reconciled periodically against Amazon’s FBA inventory ledger and adjustment reports.

Inventory Reconciliation Data

The reconciliation should compare:

  • Opening FBA inventory
  • Inbound shipment receipts
  • Customer shipments
  • Customer returns
  • Fulfillment-center transfers
  • Lost or damaged units
  • Disposals
  • Removal orders
  • Amazon reimbursements
  • Closing FBA inventory

Confirmed differences should create the appropriate SAP inventory or financial adjustment.

Without regular reconciliation, the quantity recorded as Amazon consignment stock in SAP will gradually differ from the quantity Amazon is actually holding.

Amazon Settlement and Payment Reconciliation

Amazon commonly settles merchant proceeds on an approximately 14-day cycle, although timing can vary by marketplace, account status, reserves, and transaction type.

A settlement can include:

  • Product sales
  • Customer refunds
  • Amazon commissions
  • FBA fulfillment fees
  • Storage charges
  • Removal and disposal fees
  • Advertising charges
  • Promotional costs
  • Inventory reimbursements
  • Account reserves
  • Other adjustments

The amount deposited into the bank is therefore not a simple total of customer invoices. It is a net settlement containing several transaction types.

Automated Amazon Settlement Reconciliation

A scalable Amazon FBA SAP S4 HANA integration should:

  1. Import Amazon settlement data.
  2. Match sales against SAP consignment issues and invoices.
  3. Match returns against credit memos.
  4. Post fees to the correct general-ledger accounts.
  5. Identify reimbursements and inventory adjustments.
  6. Clear the Amazon customer account.
  7. Highlight unmatched differences for review.

If order-level fees are mapped correctly, most transaction-level amounts can reconcile automatically.

Charges That May Require Separate Postings

Some account-level charges may not be directly linked to individual customer orders:

  • Monthly FBA storage fees
  • Aged-inventory surcharges
  • Advertising expenses
  • Removal and disposal fees
  • Reserve adjustments
  • Other account-level deductions

These exceptions remain manageable when the main sales, return, fee, reimbursement, and settlement flows are automated.

Benefits of Amazon FBA SAP S4 HANA Integration

True FBA Inventory Visibility

The business always knows how much inventory is sitting at Amazon. Special stock indicator W keeps this inventory visible, correctly classified, and valued on the balance sheet.

Accurate Revenue Recognition

Revenue is recognised when the customer purchase creates a consignment issue, not when inventory is sent to Amazon in bulk.

Automated Accounts Receivable

Sales, returns, credit memos, fees, and reimbursements can flow into SAP without large volumes of manual journal entries.

Reconcilable Marketplace Tax

Ship-to-driven statistical tax can be compared with Amazon’s Marketplace Facilitator reporting.

Reliable Inventory Accounting

Amazon-held inventory remains visible as a merchant-owned asset without being confused with stock physically available in the merchant’s warehouse.

Faster Financial Close

Automated settlement matching reduces the time finance teams spend investigating payouts, fees, refunds, and unexplained differences.

Exception-Based Operations

Finance and operations teams can focus on genuine exceptions instead of manually processing every Amazon transaction.

Scalability

The same closed-loop integration can support 100 orders per day or 10,000 orders per day without requiring a proportional increase in finance and operations headcount.

Amazon FBA SAP S4 HANA Integration Process Summary

The complete process works as a closed loop:

  1. Consignment fill-up: Inventory moves to Amazon without revenue recognition.
  2. Consignment issue: A customer sale consumes FBA inventory and creates revenue and receivables.
  3. Consignment return: Eligible customer returns restore Amazon consignment stock and create credit adjustments.
  4. Consignment pick-up: Unsold inventory returns to the merchant’s warehouse.
  5. Inventory reconciliation: Lost, damaged, disposed, transferred, and reimbursed units are recorded.
  6. Settlement reconciliation: Sales, fees, returns, reimbursements, and reserves are matched to Amazon payouts.

Conclusion

Amazon FBA SAP S4 HANA integration is best designed around SAP’s customer consignment cycle.

The consignment fill-up moves stock to Amazon without recognising revenue. The consignment issue records the customer sale. Consignment returns and pick-ups keep inventory and receivables aligned. Inventory and settlement reconciliation connect SAP records with what Amazon is physically holding and financially paying.

Getting the customer master, consignment order types, ASIN mappings, ship-to information, fee conditions, tax logic, and reconciliation rules right creates a scalable foundation for Amazon growth.

The result combines Amazon’s fulfillment network with SAP S/4HANA’s inventory control and financial discipline—without losing visibility over inventory the merchant still owns.

In the next part of this series, we will cover implementation best practices and the most common mistakes businesses make when integrating Amazon FBM and FBA with SAP S/4HANA.

Frequently Asked Questions About Amazon FBA SAP S4 HANA Integration

Here are some questions that are usually asked. 

What is the difference between Amazon FBA and FBM in SAP?

With FBA, inventory is sent to Amazon in bulk and Amazon fulfills customer orders. It is best modelled as a consignment process.

With FBM, the merchant stores and ships each order. It normally follows the standard SAP order-to-cash process.

Why is Amazon FBA treated as consignment in SAP?

Inventory stored at Amazon remains the merchant’s property until a customer buys it. Amazon has physical custody, but ownership has not transferred.

What are the main SAP consignment steps for Amazon FBA?

The four main steps are consignment fill-up, consignment issue, consignment return, and consignment pick-up. Not all of them will be applicable. 

When is revenue recognised for an Amazon FBA sale?

Revenue is recognised when the customer purchases the product and SAP creates the consignment issue. Revenue is not recognised when inventory is initially sent to Amazon.

What is special stock indicator W in SAP?

Special stock indicator W identifies customer consignment stock. It keeps Amazon-held inventory visible as merchant-owned inventory while separating it from normal unrestricted stock.

Does sending inventory to Amazon create an invoice?

No. A consignment fill-up represents an inventory movement, not a customer sale. It should not create revenue or a standard customer invoice.

Who should be the Sold-To Party and Payer?

Amazon typically remains the Sold-To Party and Payer because Amazon settles the merchant’s proceeds. The end customer’s address is used as the Ship-To information.

Why is the customer ship-to address required?

The customer’s delivery address determines the tax jurisdiction. SAP needs this information to calculate and reconcile tax with Amazon’s Marketplace Facilitator reporting.

How should Amazon-collected sales tax be recorded?

It is often recorded as a statistical condition. This keeps the tax visible for reconciliation without treating it as tax the merchant must remit where Amazon is responsible.

How are Amazon FBA customer returns handled in SAP?

A sellable customer return creates a consignment return, return delivery, inventory movement back into special stock W, and a credit memo linked to the original transaction.

What happens when a returned product is unsellable?

The Amazon disposition should determine whether the unit requires an inventory adjustment, reimbursement, disposal, or removal order instead of returning to sellable consignment stock.

What happens to unsold Amazon FBA inventory?

The merchant can create a removal order in Seller Central. SAP then processes a consignment pick-up when Amazon returns the units.

How should lost or damaged FBA inventory be handled?

SAP consignment inventory should be reconciled with Amazon’s FBA inventory reports. Confirmed lost, damaged, disposed, or reimbursed units should create appropriate inventory and financial adjustments.

How often does Amazon pay FBA merchants?

Amazon commonly settles merchant proceeds approximately every 14 days. The timing can vary by marketplace, account, reserve requirements, and transaction type.

Why does the Amazon payout not equal total product sales?

The payout is net of customer refunds, commissions, fulfillment fees, storage charges, advertising costs, reserves, reimbursements, and other adjustments.

Can Amazon settlement reconciliation be fully automated?

Most order-related reconciliation can be automated when sales, returns, fees, and reimbursements are mapped correctly. Some account-level charges may still require separate postings or review.

Can the integration support high Amazon order volumes?

Yes. A scalable integration uses automated document creation, API processing, scheduled reconciliation, and exception management. The same consignment design can support hundreds or thousands of daily orders.

entry.title: Accelerate Business Transformation with SAP Activate and the Agent-led Toolchain
entry.content:
Successful business transformation requires the right methodology, the right tools, and the right skills. The BTM live session programme for H2 2026 brings together SAP Activate fundamentals, workstream deep dives, certification preparation, and intensive hands-on bootcamps — all designed to help consultants, project managers, and transformation leads deliver faster, more predictable outcomes.

The hands-on bootcamps place a particular focus on the agent-led toolchain — the integrated use of SAP Signavio, SAP LeanIX, and WalkMe alongside SAP Activate — for both new business transformation programmes and SAP S/4HANA system conversions.

What You Will Learn

  • Core SAP Activate methodology — phases, workstreams, and key deliverables.
  • How to apply the agent-led toolchain across the full transformation lifecycle.
  • Certification preparation for SAP Activate Project Manager and S/4HANA Project Systems.
  • Practical bootcamp exercises covering both business transformation and system conversion scenarios.

entry.title: AI-Driven SAP Performance Insights
entry.content:
All my fellow SAP Basis admins know how useful the HANA Expensive Statements view in DBACOCKPIT can be.

It gives us detailed insight into performance-critical information like execution duration, CPU usage, memory consumption, application source, work process details, and much more. It is one of those views we often rely on when troubleshooting database performance issues.

But there is a challenge we all run into: the data does not stay around for long.

In a typical SAP S/4HANA system, expensive statement history is usually short-lived — often only around 6–8 hours in practical scenarios . So if an issue is reported later in the day, the next morning, or after a month-end process, the most useful evidence may already be gone.

That raises important questions:

 

  • When did the issue actually start?
  • How did the query perform yesterday or during month-end close?
  • Which users executed it in the past month?
  • What parameters made it expensive compared with normal executions?

 

With everything AI can do today, I started thinking: why not use AI to overcome the limitation of short-lived HANA expensive statement data — and make the analysis easier for both technical and functional teams?

That idea led to the HANA Expensive Statement Analyzer, an application designed to convert short-lived HANA expensive statement data into historical, searchable, natural-language insights

Solution Overview

The idea behind this solution is simple: capture the expensive statement data before it disappears, store it historically, and then use AI to make that data easier to analyze.

At a high level, the solution is built in three layers:

Layer 1: Data Collection and Persistence

An ABAP program periodically reads data from the HANA expensive statements source i.e. M_EXPENSIVE_STATEMENTS, and stores the relevant details in a custom SAP table.

A CDS view is then built on top of this table to structure and expose the historical data in a reusable way. This creates the foundation for analyzing expensive statements beyond the short retention window normally available in DBACOCKPIT.

Layer 2: API Exposure

The CDS-based data model is exposed through OData and made available as an API using SAP BTP AppRouter.

This layer helps securely expose the historical expensive statement data so it can be consumed by downstream applications or AI-based tools.

Layer 3: AI-Powered Natural Language Analysis

The API is then connected to an enterprise AI assistant, with a system prompt designed to help users ask meaningful performance-related questions in natural language.

For example, instead of manually filtering through historical records, users can ask questions like:

 

  • What were the top expensive statements today?
  • When did this query first become expensive?
  • Which time window had the highest database load last week?
  • Compare normal versus expensive executions for this statement.
  • Were there any expensive queries during month-end close?
  • Summarize performance issues for the last 30 days in business terms.

 

This makes the solution useful not only for SAP Basis administrators, but also for SAP developers and functional teams. Administrators can troubleshoot using current and historical data, developers can review expensive queries without recreating the issue, and business teams can ask performance questions in plain language.

A Look at the Tool in Action

Let’s take a quick look at how the tool works and how it can be useful for different personas — SAP Administrators, Developers, and Functional teams.

Below are a few sample prompts that show how each persona can use the tool in day-to-day performance analysis.

SAP Administrators

In the example below, I asked the tool to list the top 5 SQL statements by memory consumption for the day.

Along with returning the top 5 statements, the tool also provides useful observations such as:

 

  • Which statements consumed the most memory
  • Whether the statements belong to the same functional area
  • Whether the same statement hash appeared multiple times
  • Which user or application pattern may need further investigation
  • Whether the issue looks like a repeated expensive query pattern

 

This gives SAP Administrators a quick starting point for troubleshooting. Instead of only looking at raw expensive statement records, they can immediately see summarized insights that help identify users, execution times, application sources, peak load windows, and possible patterns for deeper analysis .

Sample prompt:

What are the top 5 SQLs by memory todayrsonal Favorite: Finding Busy and Idle Windows

 

One of my favorite examples is asking the tool to analyze a longer period of performance data and identify system usage trends.

Instead of looking at one expensive statement at a time, I can ask a broader question like:

Based on performance data for July, considering both memory and CPU, can you build a trend of the busiest times and idle times in the system?

This is where the historical data becomes really powerful. Since the expensive statement data is captured and stored over time, the tool can analyze patterns across days, weeks, or business cycles instead of being limited to the short-lived DBACOCKPIT history.

These are just a couple of examples, but the opportunities are endless. By combining historical expensive statement data with AI-powered natural-language analysis, SAP Administrators can quickly identify patterns, peak load windows, recurring expensive queries, and areas that need deeper investigation

SAP Developers

SAP Developers can use the tool to quickly identify expensive SQL statements originating from custom programs, CDS views, or application sources. Instead of waiting to recreate the issue or collect new traces, developers can analyze historical executions, compare expensive versus normal runs, and identify recurring patterns that may point to optimization opportunities.

For example, a developer can ask:

Can you list the top 5 memory-consuming statements for June 2026 originating from Z/custom application sources?

The tool can then summarize the most expensive custom/Z-originated statements, highlight memory and CPU usage, show repeated statement hashes, and provide key observations that help developers decide where to focus tuning efforts.

This helps developers move faster from “which query is expensive?” to “what custom object or pattern should I investigate and optimize?

 

SAP Functional Teams

Functional teams can use the tool as a self-service capability to get quick insights into expensive queries related to their business areas. Instead of waiting for detailed technical analysis, they can ask performance questions in natural language and understand whether a performance issue may be related to user selections, business process volume, reporting filters, or other functional factors within their purview .

By reviewing the parameters passed to a query, functional teams can often identify whether the issue is caused by broad selections, missing filters, large date ranges, high-volume business scenarios, or unusual usage patterns. This helps them validate the business context before engaging SAP Administrators or Developers for deeper technical troubleshooting.

For example, a functional user can ask:

Based on July performance data, list the top 5 expensive queries by memory in the finance area.

The tool can summarize the most expensive queries for that business area, highlight related objects, and provide tuning-focused observations that can help functional teams collaborate more effectively with Basis and development teams.

This makes performance analysis more accessible beyond technical teams and helps move troubleshooting from “technical data only” to a shared conversation between functional, Basis, and development teams.

 

Conclusion

AI gives SAP Administrators a powerful opportunity to simplify routine analysis, reduce manual effort, and focus more on complex performance challenges. By combining historical HANA expensive statement data with natural-language analysis, we can move beyond short-lived monitoring and turn technical performance data into actionable insights.

This helps SAP Administrators, Developers, and Functional teams troubleshoot faster, identify recurring patterns, and collaborate more effectively when resolving performance issues.

Using AI in the right way allows us to add more value to the business — solving complex problems with better context, faster turnaround time, and more meaningful insights.
